question: Define an exterior point of a subset A in a metric space (X, d).
expectedPoints: A point x ∈ X is an exterior point of A if there exists an open ball centered at x that is completely contained in the complement of A.
Formally: There exists some r > 0 such that B(x, r) ⊆ A^c.
An exterior point of A is an interior point of the complement of A.

question: Prove that the set of exterior points of a set A is always open.
expectedPoints: Let Ext(A) denote the set of exterior points of A.
By definition, Ext(A) is the set of interior points of the complement, A^c. That is, Ext(A) = int(A^c).
It is a fundamental property that the interior of any set in a metric space is an open set.

question: Let A be a subset of a metric space (X, d). Prove that the boundary of A, ∂A, is the intersection of the closure of A and the closure of its complement.
expectedPoints: Recall the definition: x ∈ ∂A if and only if every open ball centered at x intersects both A and its complement, A^c.
This is equivalent to saying x is a limit point of both A and A^c.
Therefore, x is in the closure of A (x ∈ Ā) because every ball around x contains a point of A.
Similarly, x is in the closure of A^c (x ∈ A^c‾).
Conversely, if x ∈ Ā ∩ A^c‾, then every ball around x contains a point from A (since x ∈ Ā) and a point from A^c (since x ∈ A^c‾).
Therefore, x ∈ ∂A, proving that Ā ∩ A^c‾ ⊆ ∂A.
